prompt:
Our month-end filing script has started lying to us. snapshot.sh copies the loose files out of the finance drop folder into a labelled snapshot directory, writes a MANIFEST.tsv, and then re-checks its own work. It's been fine for months — turns out 'fine' meant 'fine as long as everything is named like readme.txt'. This month the drop has the finance team's actual exports in it — 'Monthly Report $Q3.txt', 'budget (final) v2.csv', 'notes [draft].md' — and the run is a train wreck:

    $ bash snapshot.sh drop snaps 2026-06
    cp: cannot stat 'Monthly': No such file or directory
    cp: cannot stat 'budget': No such file or directory
    ... more of the same, for names I never typed ...
    snapshot: missing from snapshot: Monthly Report $Q3.txt
    snapshot: missing from snapshot: budget (final) v2.csv
    copied 1 file(s) into snaps/2026-06
    verify FAILED: 4 file(s) did not make it

It copied ONE file out of five, cp is complaining about files that don't exist under names that are fragments of the real ones, the manifest is nearly empty, and the paranoia pass at the end (correctly, for once) says most of the drop never arrived. Nobody is going to rename their files to suit a shell script — these are perfectly ordinary document names. Please fix snapshot.sh so a drop full of real-world filenames copies completely, the manifest is right, and the verify pass is quiet. Keep the output format and the refuse-to-overwrite behavior exactly as they are; downstream tooling reads both. bash test_ledgersnap.sh is the regression gate — it must pass unmodified, and don't touch the test file.
